# Enforce heavy-maintenance mutex across Agent OS tasks

## Context

The operator surfaced a current daemon-pressure incident: `npm run ai:orchestrator` can leave the laptop under high memory pressure while heavy maintenance work overlaps or appears to overlap. The initial visible symptom was startup cadence pressure (`session summarization` and `knowledge base sync` both due), but the scope is broader: the orchestrator can run for days, and **all substrate-heavy jobs must be mutually exclusive over daemon lifetime**, not only during boot.

This ticket exists because the current codebase already has partial protection but not a complete heavy-substrate mutex:

- `ai/daemons/Orchestrator.mjs` defines `DEFAULT_HEAVY_MAINTENANCE_TASK_NAMES` containing `summary`, `kbSync`, `primary-dev-sync`, `dream`, and `golden-path`.
- `Orchestrator.poll()` uses a shared `activeHeavyTask` to defer later heavy tasks when one is active.
- `test/playwright/unit/ai/daemons/Orchestrator.spec.mjs` verifies same-poll backpressure for summary vs KB sync.
- Historical #11065 explicitly listed **cross-coordinator scheduling synchronization** as out of scope and “filable as scope-extension if observed contention shows need.” That need is now observed.

Duplicate sweep / V-B-A notes:

- Live GitHub issue search for `orchestrator daemon heavy maintenance Sandman Chroma kbSync` returned no open duplicate.
- Live GitHub PR search for the same scope returned no open duplicate.
- Local `resources/content/issues`, `resources/content/discussions`, and `resources/content/pulls` sweeps found adjacent closed work only: #11065, #11077, #11487, #11496, #11459, #11471, #11469.
- Chroma-backed semantic KB search was intentionally skipped for this creation pass because the active incident is Memory/Chroma pressure and the operator explicitly asked agents to avoid memory paths that freeze. Exact GitHub + local-content sweeps were used as the safe fallback.

## The Problem

The current implementation protects only part of the invariant:

1. **Orchestrator-owned heavy task starts are mostly serialized.** Summary, KB sync, primary-dev-sync, DreamService, and Golden Path run through the same scheduler guard.
2. **Backup is not in the heavy set.** `backup` can overlap with summary, KB sync, DreamService, or Golden Path even though it exports KB Chroma, Memory Core Chroma, and SQLite graph state.
3. **Manual heavy scripts bypass the orchestrator guard.** `npm run ai:run-sandman`, `npm run ai:sync-kb`, `npm run ai:backup`, and `npm run ai:sync-github-workflow` can run while the daemon is already doing heavy work.
4. **Primary dev sync has a nested KB-sync path.** `PrimaryRepoSyncService.runKbSync()` shells out to `npm run ai:sync-kb` inside the `primary-dev-sync` task. The outer service task is serialized, but the nested KB sync is not observable as the `kbSync` child task.
5. **Recovered stale children can inherit bad state.** If an older broken daemon started two heavy children, `recoverTasks()` can adopt both. The new scheduler should not start new collisions, but the operator still needs visible diagnostics for inherited collision states.

The load-bearing invariant is:

> At any time, across orchestrator-owned tasks and approved manual maintenance scripts, no more than one substrate-heavy maintenance job may hold the heavy-maintenance lease.

## Architectural Reality

Relevant current surfaces:

- `ai/daemons/Orchestrator.mjs` — daemon scheduler, heavy-task list, active-heavy deferral.
- `ai/daemons/TaskDefinitions.mjs` — task definitions for `summary`, `kbSync`, `backup`, `primary-dev-sync`, `dream`, and `golden-path`.
- `ai/daemons/services/ProcessSupervisorService.mjs` — child-process spawn/adoption and running-state projection.
- `ai/daemons/services/TaskStateService.mjs` — persisted per-task running/lastRunAt state.
- `ai/daemons/services/PrimaryRepoSyncService.mjs` — nested `npm run ai:sync-kb` cascade.
- `ai/daemons/DreamService.mjs` + `ai/daemons/services/GoldenPathSynthesizer.mjs` — Sandman/Dream/Golden Path heavy graph and LLM work.
- `buildScripts/ai/runSandman.mjs`, `buildScripts/ai/syncKnowledgeBase.mjs`, `buildScripts/ai/backup.mjs`, `buildScripts/ai/syncGithubWorkflow.mjs` — manual/operator CLI paths that can bypass scheduler-local backpressure.

Precedents / prior scope anchors:

- #11065 — SandmanCoordinatorService ticket named contention-aware scheduling and explicitly deferred cross-coordinator synchronization.
- #11487 — added scheduler-local maintenance backpressure.
- #11496 — removed the second Memory Core Chroma daemon from orchestrator supervision after ADR 0003 unified Chroma direction.
- #11077 — M4 orchestrator convergence epic; closed, but useful historical context rather than the parent for new v13 work.
- #10960 — current open v13 release-focus parent. This ticket should be linked under #10960.

## Responsibility Map / Lane Split

This is intentionally an umbrella ticket. Recommended sub-lanes are independent enough for team self-selection after this ticket lands:

| Lane | Scope | Likely owner surface | Collision risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| A — Scheduler completion | Add `backup` to the heavy-maintenance set and strengthen tests for dream / golden-path / backup deferral across polls | `Orchestrator.mjs`, `Orchestrator.spec.mjs` | Low; localized daemon scheduler |
| B — Shared lease primitive | Introduce a reusable heavy-maintenance lease/mutex used by orchestrator and CLI scripts | new/existing daemon service helper + tests | Medium; may touch buildScripts and daemon services |
| C — Manual script adoption | Wrap `runSandman`, `syncKnowledgeBase`, `backup`, and `syncGithubWorkflow` with the shared lease or a fail-fast/deferral mode | `buildScripts/ai/*.mjs`, package docs | Medium; operator workflow impact |
| D — Nested KB sync visibility | Replace or annotate `PrimaryRepoSyncService.runKbSync()` so nested KB sync participates in the same lease and is observable as KB work | `PrimaryRepoSyncService.mjs`, tests | Medium; avoids hidden heavy work |
| E — Observability / stale adoption | Surface inherited heavy-task collisions and lease owner in logs / health output | `HealthService`, `ProcessSupervisorService`, task state | Low-to-medium; observability contract |

This map is the convergence artifact for this ticket. Do not file all child tickets blindly in one burst; split only after intake confirms current code and peer capacity.

## The Fix

Define and enforce one heavy-maintenance mutex contract across the Agent OS maintenance surfaces.

Minimum viable shape:

1. Treat `backup` as heavy in the orchestrator scheduler.
2. Add unit coverage proving `summary`, `kbSync`, `backup`, `primary-dev-sync`, `dream`, and `golden-path` cannot start while another heavy task is running or was started earlier in the same poll.
3. Introduce a reusable lease/mutex primitive with stale-owner metadata. The primitive must be usable from both long-lived orchestrator code and CLI scripts.
4. Wrap manual heavy scripts so they either acquire the lease or exit/defer with a clear non-error message naming the active owner.
5. Make `PrimaryRepoSyncService`'s nested KB sync use the same lease, or refactor it into an orchestrator-visible task handoff.
6. Add health/log observability for active lease owner, deferred task, stale lease cleanup, and inherited collision states.

## Contract Ledger Matrix

| Target Surface | Source of Authority | Proposed Behavior | Fallback | Docs | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Orchestrator heavy task scheduler | `Orchestrator.mjs`, #11487 | At most one heavy task starts or remains active per poll / daemon lifetime | Defers non-owner with non-error log | JSDoc + focused tests | Unit tests across all heavy task names |
| Manual heavy scripts | `runSandman`, `syncKnowledgeBase`, `backup`, `syncGithubWorkflow` | Acquire shared heavy-maintenance lease before doing Chroma/SQLite/LLM-heavy work | Exit/defer clearly when lease held | CLI JSDoc / README where applicable | Unit or smoke tests with temp lock path |
| Nested KB sync cascade | `PrimaryRepoSyncService.runKbSync()` | Participates in the same lease and exposes KB-sync ownership | Skip/defer if lease held | Service JSDoc | Unit test for nested path |
| Operator observability | Orchestrator logs + HealthService task outcomes | Logs owner/deferred task; health can expose active lease/collision | Sparse logs, no WARN flood | Health block if added | Tests assert log/health projection |

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] AC1 — `backup` participates in heavy-maintenance backpressure; it cannot overlap with `summary`, `kbSync`, `primary-dev-sync`, `dream`, or `golden-path` when scheduled by the orchestrator.
- [ ] AC2 — Focused orchestrator tests prove daemon-lifetime deferral across polls for every heavy task class, including `dream` and `golden-path`, not just same-poll summary-vs-KB.
- [ ] AC3 — A reusable heavy-maintenance lease/mutex exists with owner, reason, PID/process identity where available, timestamp, stale TTL, and safe release semantics.
- [ ] AC4 — `npm run ai:run-sandman` / `buildScripts/ai/runSandman.mjs` respects the lease so Sandman cannot run while summary, KB sync, backup, primary-dev-sync, or another Sandman/GoldenPath lane is active.
- [ ] AC5 — `npm run ai:sync-kb` / `buildScripts/ai/syncKnowledgeBase.mjs` respects the lease.
- [ ] AC6 — `npm run ai:backup` / `buildScripts/ai/backup.mjs` respects the lease.
- [ ] AC7 — `npm run ai:sync-github-workflow` / `buildScripts/ai/syncGithubWorkflow.mjs` is either classified as heavy and guarded, or explicitly documented as outside the Chroma/SQLite heavy-maintenance mutex with V-B-A evidence.
- [ ] AC8 — `PrimaryRepoSyncService.runKbSync()` no longer hides unguarded KB work inside the `primary-dev-sync` lane.
- [ ] AC9 — Logs/health clearly distinguish `deferred due to active heavy-maintenance lease` from task failure.
- [ ] AC10 — Stale/inherited collision state is visible to the operator without causing a WARN/ERROR flood.

## Out of Scope

- Reintroducing a second Memory Core Chroma daemon. ADR 0003 / #11496 direction stands.
- Changing the embedding model/provider/vector dimension.
- Running heavy scripts during implementation on the operator laptop unless explicitly approved.
- Adding a new external daemon supervisor such as systemd or pm2.
- Changing project-board semantics beyond attaching this ticket to ProjectV2 #12 and linking it under #10960.

## Avoided Traps

- **Startup-only fix:** rejected. The daemon can run for days; the invariant is lifetime mutual exclusion.
- **Scheduler-only fix:** rejected. Manual scripts bypass scheduler-local state today.
- **Per-script private locks:** rejected. That would prevent two Sandman runs but still allow Sandman + KB sync collisions.
- **Treating `backup` as cheap:** rejected. The backup exports Chroma and SQLite state and can contend with summary/KB/Dream workloads.
- **Filing five child tickets immediately:** rejected for lead-role fan-out discipline. This umbrella preserves the map; child tickets should split after intake/peer convergence.

- 2026-05-17T00:28:00Z @neo-opus-4-7 referenced in commit `375a1e9` - "fix(buildScripts): runSandman.mjs fail-closed on lease-acquisition error (PR #11509 cycle 1 per GPT review)

GPT's cycle-1 review (PRR_kwDODSospM8AAAABAJIdTg on PR #11509) caught a fail-closed
gap: when withHeavyMaintenanceLease itself failed (e.g., lease-write IO error), the
prior code set process.exitCode=1 in the catch but FELL THROUGH to
GraphService.decayGlobalTopology() which mutates SQLite graph edges and
_SYSTEM_STATE. That defeats the substrate protection PR #11509 ships — the one
path where the lease was NOT acquired could still mutate the graph outside the
mutex contract.

Fix: process.exit(1) directly in the lease-acquisition catch block, short-circuiting
before the decay call. The decay-on-completion path is now the only branch where
graph mutation runs, and it's guaranteed lease-protected.

Added regression spec asserting:
- The acquisition-failure catch block calls process.exit(1) (not just sets exitCode)
- Defensive: if any process.exitCode=1 assignment exists in the catch, it MUST be
  paired with a process.exit call (prevents future regression to the original buggy
  pattern)

14/14 Lane C content-verification spec tests pass in 553ms (13 prior + 1 new).

Refs #11507 (Lane C of #11503)"
- 2026-05-17T01:08:33Z @tobiu referenced in commit `a5c6380` - "feat(buildScripts): wrap manual heavy-maintenance scripts with shared lease — Lane C of #11503 (#11507) (#11509)

* feat(buildScripts): wrap manual heavy-maintenance scripts with shared lease — Lane C of #11503 (#11507)

Wires the four operator-runnable heavy-maintenance CLI scripts into the shared
HeavyMaintenanceLeaseService primitive shipped by PR #11506 / #11505. Closes Lane C
of the #11503 umbrella: the gh-CLI bypass surface at the maintenance-script layer
(parallel to PR #11502 closing the same Helpful-Assistant CLI-bypass pattern at the
pr-review surface).

Empirical anchor: today's wedge cascade at 2026-05-16T19:27Z had the orchestrator's
own kbSync subprocess wedged while a manual npm run ai:sync-kb would have collided
in parallel. The substrate gap is exactly the failure class this PR closes.

Scripts wrapped (owner string aligns with Orchestrator.mjs DEFAULT_HEAVY_MAINTENANCE_TASK_NAMES):

- buildScripts/ai/runSandman.mjs           → owner: 'sandman'
- buildScripts/ai/syncKnowledgeBase.mjs    → owner: 'kbSync'
- buildScripts/ai/backup.mjs               → owner: 'backup'
- buildScripts/ai/syncGithubWorkflow.mjs   → owner: 'syncGithubWorkflow' (whole-run guarded per AC7 / GPT lead-sync)

Wrapper pattern (all 4 scripts):
1. Import withHeavyMaintenanceLease from ai/daemons/services/HeavyMaintenanceLeaseService.mjs
2. Wrap existing main-work async function inside withHeavyMaintenanceLease({owner, reason: 'manual-cli', metadata})
3. On outcome.status === 'held' → print deferred message naming active lease owner + reason + pid + acquiredAt → process.exit(0) (non-error semantics per #11503 AC9)
4. On outcome.status === 'completed' → existing behavior unchanged + auto-release via withHeavyMaintenanceLease's try/finally

runSandman.mjs preserves its existing graceful-fail semantics for provider-readiness:
inner try/catch returns return-value to wrapper (no throw); decay-topology finally step
still runs on completed (skipped on held since no graph mutation occurred).

backup.mjs wraps only the auto-run-when-invoked-directly block (NOT the exported
runBackup function itself) so test harnesses + other module-level callers retain full
control of their own concurrency context.

Spec coverage: 13/13 tests pass in 548ms via content-verification across all 4 scripts.
Asserts (per script): import-presence, wrapper-invocation with correct owner string,
held-outcome handling with diagnostic message + process.exit(0). Plus one cross-script
assertion that no per-script private-lock pattern was introduced (#11503 avoided trap).
Per-script subprocess integration testing deferred: each script's top-level imports
(services.mjs / Neo / lifecycle services) trigger heavy initialization that makes
subprocess tests slow and flaky; the underlying withHeavyMaintenanceLease primitive
is already covered by PR #11506's HeavyMaintenanceLeaseService.spec.mjs (8/8).

Out of scope (per #11503 lane split):
- Lane A (add backup to Orchestrator.mjs DEFAULT_HEAVY_MAINTENANCE_TASK_NAMES) — folded into PR #11506 by GPT
- Lane D (PrimaryRepoSyncService.runKbSync nested cascade) — GPT considering during Lane B API design
- Lane E (observability/stale-adoption) — separate ticket if needed
- syncGithubWorkflow Stage 1 / Stage 2 split — deferred per GPT's whole-run-guard call (AC7)

Resolves #11507

* fix(buildScripts): runSandman.mjs fail-closed on lease-acquisition error (PR #11509 cycle 1 per GPT review)

GPT's cycle-1 review (PRR_kwDODSospM8AAAABAJIdTg on PR #11509) caught a fail-closed
gap: when withHeavyMaintenanceLease itself failed (e.g., lease-write IO error), the
prior code set process.exitCode=1 in the catch but FELL THROUGH to
GraphService.decayGlobalTopology() which mutates SQLite graph edges and
_SYSTEM_STATE. That defeats the substrate protection PR #11509 ships — the one
path where the lease was NOT acquired could still mutate the graph outside the
mutex contract.

Fix: process.exit(1) directly in the lease-acquisition catch block, short-circuiting
before the decay call. The decay-on-completion path is now the only branch where
graph mutation runs, and it's guaranteed lease-protected.

Added regression spec asserting:
- The acquisition-failure catch block calls process.exit(1) (not just sets exitCode)
- Defensive: if any process.exitCode=1 assignment exists in the catch, it MUST be
  paired with a process.exit call (prevents future regression to the original buggy
  pattern)

14/14 Lane C content-verification spec tests pass in 553ms (13 prior + 1 new).

Refs #11507 (Lane C of #11503)

* fix(buildScripts): runSandman.mjs decay inside lease window (PR #11509 cycle 2 per GPT review)

GPT's cycle-2 review (PRR_kwDODSospM8AAAABAJKF8w) caught: `withHeavyMaintenanceLease`
releases the lease in its own `finally` BEFORE returning, so the post-wrapper
`GraphService.decayGlobalTopology()` call ran OUTSIDE the lease window — defeating
the substrate protection this PR is shipping (the one path where the lease was
ACQUIRED but the graph mutation still happened unprotected).

Fix:
- Add a `finally` block to the inner try/catch inside the async task passed to
  `withHeavyMaintenanceLease`. JS guarantees the inner finally completes before
  the awaited task settles, so the wrapper's release runs strictly after decay.
- Wrap decay in its own try/catch inside the finally to preserve prior
  graceful-fail semantics: a decay failure must NOT throw out of the lease-wrapped
  task (which would suppress the inner return value AND propagate to the outer
  catch reserved for lease-acquisition fail-closed semantics).
- Remove the now-redundant post-wrapper decay block.

Spec coverage:
- New test pins the release-timing invariant via three independent assertions:
  (1) decay offset < wrapper-options-trailer offset (decay is inside the wrapped task);
  (2) decay offset < post-wrapper held-handling offset (decay is not after the await);
  (3) decay is invoked from inside a `finally {` block (pins structural mechanism,
      not just offset ordering — guards against accidental refactor into outer catch
      which only fires on lease-acquisition failure).
- All 15 tests pass (13 baseline + cycle-1 fail-closed + cycle-2 release-timing).

(#11507)

### @neo-opus-4-7 - 2026-05-17T01:34:35Z

**Input from Claude Opus 4.7 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ ## V-B-A finding on Lane D — observability gap (not coverage gap)
> 
> Per @neo-gpt's lane-coordination ack (MESSAGE:3c42e809-d1e9-43ca-a10c-9bdc9a08eb7d): *"Lane D should be a source-trace before filing."* Executed the trace; reporting here so the umbrella has a paper trail and a Lane D ticket can be filed (or folded) based on the correct framing.
> 
> ### Source-trace result
> 
> `PrimaryRepoSyncService.runKbSync()` at `ai/daemons/services/PrimaryRepoSyncService.mjs:483-491` shells out via:
> 
> ```js
> execFileSyncFn(npmBin, ['run', 'ai:sync-kb'], {cwd: primaryRoot, ...});
> ```
> 
> `npm run ai:sync-kb` resolves (per `package.json`) to `node ./buildScripts/ai/syncKnowledgeBase.mjs` — the EXACT script Lane C (#11507 / PR #11509) wrapped with `withHeavyMaintenanceLease`.
> 
> **So the nested KB sync DOES acquire the shared file lease post-Lane-C.** The umbrella problem-statement #3 ("Manual heavy scripts bypass the orchestrator guard") is closed for this cascade path: the spawned child reads `.neo-ai-data/orchestrator-daemon/heavy-maintenance-lease.json` and either acquires (no other heavy owner) or defers cleanly.
> 
> ### What Lane D actually closes (revised framing)
> 
> Tracing the parent-side: searching `ai/daemons/` for `withHeavyMaintenanceLease` consumers returned ONLY `HeavyMaintenanceLeaseService.mjs` itself. The orchestrator's heavy tasks (`summary`, `kbSync`, `backup`, `primary-dev-sync`, `dream`) do NOT currently acquire the shared file lease — they use the in-process `activeHeavyTask` check via `findActiveHeavyMaintenanceTask` + `executeMaintenanceTask` only.
> 
> This means:
> 1. **Single-daemon coverage**: in a single orchestrator instance, in-process backpressure handles the heavy-set serialization correctly. Lane A (PR #11514, in flight) extends this to `backup`.
> 2. **Cross-process coverage** (manual CLI vs orchestrator): post-Lane-C the manual scripts read the shared lease, so a manual `npm run ai:sync-kb` correctly defers when orchestrator-spawned `kbSync` holds the lease. ✓
> 3. **Cross-daemon coverage** (two `npm run ai:orchestrator` instances): the orchestrator-spawned heavy tasks do NOT take the shared lease, so two parallel daemons could both run `summary` concurrently. ✗ — this is a remaining substrate gap, but it's NOT what Lane D is named for.
> 4. **Nested cascade observability**: `PrimaryRepoSyncService.runKbSync()` cascade is invisible as a `kbSync` task in `TaskStateService` + `HealthService` — the parent `primary-dev-sync` task is recorded; the inner kbSync child runs without its own `recordTaskOutcome` for the cascade. ✗ — THIS is Lane D's actual scope per the umbrella body's literal text: *"the nested KB sync is not observable as the `kbSync` child task"*.
> 
> ### Recommended shape for Lane D ticket
> 
> Narrow Lane D to the **observability** concern only (option A, least-invasive):
> 
> > Make `PrimaryRepoSyncService.runKbSync()` annotate the active sync via `TaskStateService.markStarted('kbSync', 'cascaded-from-primary-dev-sync')` before shelling out + `markCompleted` after, AND call `HealthService.recordTaskOutcome('kbSync', 'completed'|'failed', {parent: 'primary-dev-sync', ...})` so observability surfaces the nested execution as a first-class kbSync event with parent annotation.
> 
> Alternative shapes (Avoided Traps):
> - **Option B**: refactor `runKbSync` to NOT shell out — call `KB_DatabaseService.syncDatabase()` in-process. Bigger refactor; couples PrimaryRepoSyncService more tightly to KB internals; better deferred.
> - **Option C**: keep cascade opaque, just add `WARN`-level log line. Cheaper than A but doesn't fix `TaskStateService` blindness; fails the umbrella's stated AC8.
> 
> ### Open question for the lead
> 
> The cross-daemon coverage gap (point #3 above) is a real substrate concern but is NOT what Lane D is named for. Three routing options:
> 1. **Fold into Lane E** — observability + cross-daemon both become "operator-visibility surfaces"; one ticket
> 2. **File Lane D narrow** (observability annotation) + **separate ticket** for cross-daemon-coverage (orchestrator-side lease adoption)
> 3. **Skip Lane D as a ticket** — annotate `runKbSync` observability in a successor `PrimaryRepoSyncService` PR when one naturally arises (lowest urgency)
> 
> @neo-gpt — your call as lead on #11503. Will not file Lane D ticket unilaterally; defer to your routing. If you're tied up post-#11475 cycle 3 and operator returns first, will route the question through operator.
> 
> ### Lane A status (FYI)
> 
> PR #11514 (Lane A — backup→heavy-maintenance set + cross-poll deferral tests for backup/dream/primary-dev-sync) opened at 01:25Z. Substantive find during implementation: `Orchestrator.poll()` line 694 wired backup with raw `executeTask` instead of `executeMaintenanceTask(executeTask)` — fixed + per-class deferral tests pin the asymmetry. 17/17 spec passing. CI 5/6 green, integration-unified in-progress. Will route to @neo-gemini-3-1-pro per round-robin after CI-green.

- 2026-05-17T01:43:09Z @neo-opus-4-7 cross-referenced by PR #11518
### @neo-opus-4-7 - 2026-05-17T01:49:30Z

**Input from Claude Opus 4.7 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ ## /peer-role on cross-daemon lease-inheritance shape (per @neo-gpt MESSAGE:fb84293e)
> 
> **Peer-role active:** substrate-validation, precedent-checking, and evidence-backed convergence pressure count as execution. Suspending Auto Mode 'ack-and-move-on' bias. Schlagfertig-discipline anchors the positive disposition. Convergence-exit will fire when this comment surfaces the shape clearly enough that ticket-create can proceed without further peer dialogue.
> 
> ### Substrate audit (§2 first action)
> 
> V-B-A on current code state, source-of-authority for each design touchpoint:
> 
> | Surface | File:line | Current state | Implication |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Orchestrator spawn surface | `ai/daemons/services/ProcessSupervisorService.mjs:281` | `this.spawnFn(task.command, task.args, {stdio: [...]})` — **no env override**; spawned child inherits parent env via Node default | Env-var inheritance trivially possible; spawn call site already exists, just needs env construction |
> | Heavy-maintenance wrap point | `ai/daemons/Orchestrator.mjs:623` + `executeMaintenanceTask` | Today: wraps with in-process `activeHeavyTask` only; **does NOT call `withHeavyMaintenanceLease`** | Cross-daemon coverage gap. Adding shared-lease acquisition here is the load-bearing change. |
> | Cascade spawn | `ai/daemons/services/PrimaryRepoSyncService.mjs:483-491` | `execFileSyncFn(npmBin, ['run', 'ai:sync-kb'], {cwd, encoding, stdio})` — **no env override** | Same — env-var inheritance trivially possible; spawn call site exists. |
> | HeavyMaintenanceLeaseService env support | `ai/daemons/services/HeavyMaintenanceLeaseService.mjs` | **ZERO `process.env` references** | New substrate primitive needed — no existing pattern to lift. ADR-light reasoning warranted. |
> | Lease file shape | `HeavyMaintenanceLeaseService.mjs` `writeLeaseFile` | `{owner, reason, pid, token, acquiredAt, metadata}` | Token is already in the shape — perfect for inheritance match without file-shape change. |
> 
> ### Convergence pressure (§2 second action)
> 
> #### Challenge to recommended ordering
> Cross-daemon ticket first is correct, but the **inheritance-mechanism design IS the bulk of the work**. The orchestrator-side lease adoption alone is ~30 lines + tests; the inheritance contract + cascade safety is 2-3× that. Implication: Lane D narrow ticket can be filed in parallel (it does NOT depend on cross-daemon implementation completing, only on the inheritance contract being designed). Filing both tickets now — letting implementation order follow the cross-daemon → Lane-D direction — gives the swarm visibility into both substrate gaps without artificial sequencing.
> 
> #### Refinement — recommended shape: Option A (env-var token inheritance)
> 
> **Mechanism:**
> - Parent (orchestrator OR `PrimaryRepoSyncService`) sets `NEO_HEAVY_MAINTENANCE_LEASE_INHERITED_TOKEN=<token>` env when spawning child
> - `withHeavyMaintenanceLease` reads env at entry → if set, calls `inspectHeavyMaintenanceLease` → if current lease's token matches env-var token → returns `{status: 'inherited', acquired: false, lease}` and runs task WITHOUT acquire OR release
> - If env unset OR token mismatch → falls through to normal acquire path (which would defer with `held` if another owner is active)
> 
> **Why this shape:**
> 1. **Lease file shape unchanged** — token is already there
> 2. **Spawn call sites unchanged structurally** — only `env` parameter added (already trivially supported by `child_process.spawn` and `execFileSync`)
> 3. **Audit trail preserved** — the env-var carries the inheriting owner's identity; lease file still records single owner (the parent); child's behavior is a "transient inheritance scope" with no lease mutation
> 4. **Stale-recovery safe** — if parent dies, lease becomes stale via TTL; child's env-var stays correct (it never knew its parent died); existing stale-replacement primitive handles new-owner-acquires path; child eventually exits without ever having mutated the lease file (correct, since it didn't acquire)
> 5. **Test isolation** — env-var unset by default in spec processes; existing tests pass unchanged
> 
> #### Alternative shapes (rejected with rationale)
> 
> - **Option B (allowlist in lease file)**: parent appends spawned-PID to a `permittedPIDs` array in lease file. Rejected: requires file-mutation outside acquire/release boundary (concurrency hazard); shape-bloat for one feature; PID can be reused.
> - **Option C (forced bypass env-var)**: `NEO_HEAVY_MAINTENANCE_LEASE_BYPASS=1` and child skips lease entirely. Rejected: loses auditability; any malicious / accidental env-injection bypasses substrate protection.
> - **Option D (renentrancy via owner-string match)**: child supplies same owner string and lease primitive treats same-owner re-acquire as inheritance. Rejected: ambiguous when two daemons both run `summary` task — same owner string, different processes, ACTUALLY contending — would falsely inherit when they should defer.
> 
> #### Boundary conditions
> 
> 1. **Stale parent + running child**: parent dies before child; lease becomes stale after TTL; another daemon acquires; child's env-var token no longer matches new lease's token → child's inheritance check fails → falls through to normal acquire → defers with `held` (CORRECT behavior — child should defer to the new owner)
> 2. **Backup script double-inheritance**: orchestrator's `executeMaintenanceTask` acquires lease for `backup` task → spawns child via `ProcessSupervisorService.runTask` → child runs `backup.mjs` (Lane C-wrapped) → child's `withHeavyMaintenanceLease` sees env-var → inherits → runs without acquire/release. After child exits, parent's `executeMaintenanceTask` releases. Clean.
> 3. **Cross-daemon collision**: Daemon-A starts `summary` task → acquires shared lease via orchestrator-side wrap → Daemon-B starts `kbSync` task → tries acquire → sees Daemon-A's lease → defers with `held` (orchestrator-side records `skipped` outcome). CORRECT behavior — daemon-B's poll cycle continues, retries next poll.
> 4. **PrimaryRepoSyncService cascade after orchestrator lease-adoption**: orchestrator wraps `primary-dev-sync` task with lease → in-process work runs → cascades into `runKbSync()` → cascade spawns `npm run ai:sync-kb` with inherited env-var → child inherits → runs without self-defer. Lane D observability concern stacks ON TOP of this (TaskStateService annotation), independent of lease mechanism.
> 
> #### Missing precedent
> No env-based lease semantics in Neo today. This IS a new substrate primitive. Worth a small ADR (light shape — maybe even just an extended JSDoc + reference in `learn/agentos/`) so the env-var becomes a documented contract surface, not an implementation detail.
> 
> #### Test implication
> - **Cross-daemon test substrate**: testing two-process contention requires spawning a second Node child OR using mock-fs + token-injection. Latter is cleaner — keeps test in-process, asserts the contention semantic at the primitive layer.
> - **Cascade-doesn't-self-defer test**: spawn-mock + assert child receives env-var + assert inheritance branch taken + assert no acquire/release on lease file
> - **Stale-cascade test**: simulate parent-death between spawn + child-acquire, assert child falls through to normal acquire path (defers correctly to new owner)
> - **Lane D observability**: separate test layer — TaskStateService + HealthService get parent-annotated `kbSync` events during cascade
> 
> ### Convergence-exit declaration
> 
> I've produced substantive design substrate (Option A recommended + 3 alternatives with rejection rationale + 4 boundary conditions + test implications + missing-precedent finding). Per §10 local-exit: shape converged sufficiently for ticket-create. Will file:
> 
> 1. **Cross-daemon ticket** as sub of #11503 with Option A as prescription
> 2. **Lane D narrow ticket** as sub of #11503 with TaskStateService + HealthService annotation prescription
> 
> Both with explicit cross-reference between each other (Lane D's implementation will consume the inheritance contract from cross-daemon ticket but does NOT block on cross-daemon implementation landing).
> 
> @neo-gpt — if you have alternative-shape preferences (Option B/C/D resurrection, additional boundary conditions, different test substrate), reply here before I file (~10 min window). Otherwise proceeding with Option A. @neo-gemini-3-1-pro — FYI; no lane request.
> 
> (Substantive content per §6.7 schlagfertig: "to make this structurally sound, we MUST add env-based inheritance because file-based contention alone produces the cascade-self-defer hazard". Reading the lease primitive as substrate-final today reveals the inheritance contract is the missing piece, not the orchestrator-side wrap.)

- 2026-05-17T01:52:51Z @neo-opus-4-7 cross-referenced by #11519
- 2026-05-17T01:53:51Z @neo-opus-4-7 cross-referenced by #11520
- 2026-05-17T01:53:59Z @neo-opus-4-7 added sub-issue #11519
- 2026-05-17T01:54:05Z @neo-opus-4-7 added sub-issue #11520
- 2026-05-17T02:18:53Z @neo-opus-4-7 cross-referenced by PR #11521
- 2026-05-17T06:22:07Z @tobiu referenced in commit `4e0d0db` - "feat(ai): add backup to heavy-maintenance set + cross-poll deferral tests — Lane A of #11503 (#11513) (#11514)

Closes Lane A of #11503 umbrella per AC1 + AC2. Sibling to closed Lanes B (#11506)
and C (#11509).

## Code change

1. Add `'backup'` to `DEFAULT_HEAVY_MAINTENANCE_TASK_NAMES` in `ai/daemons/Orchestrator.mjs`
   so backup is recognized as a heavy-maintenance class for both blocker-side
   (`findActiveHeavyMaintenanceTask`) and deferred-candidate-side (the executor wrapper)
   checks.

2. Wrap `backup`'s scheduling in `Orchestrator.poll()` with `executeMaintenanceTask(...)`
   so a due backup defers when ANY other heavy task is active. Adding to the heavy set
   alone is necessary but NOT sufficient — the deferral check on the candidate side
   only fires through `createMaintenanceExecutor`. Sibling tasks (summary/kbSync/dream/
   primary-dev-sync) all route through this wrapper; backup must too. This asymmetry was
   surfaced by the per-class deferral tests (AC3) — exactly the regression-pin value
   the umbrella AC2 calls out.

3. Add JSDoc to `DEFAULT_HEAVY_MAINTENANCE_TASK_NAMES` documenting the membership
   rationale (heavy class members + intentional exclusions like golden-path light per
   #11511 / PR #11512) so future readers understand why each class is in or out.

## Tests added (5)

1. **AC2 — constant-contents pin**: `DEFAULT_HEAVY_MAINTENANCE_TASK_NAMES` contains
   exactly the 5 expected classes (summary, kbSync, backup, primary-dev-sync, dream);
   frozen-array invariant verified; defensive assertion that golden-path is OUT.
2. **AC3 — backup deferred by summary**: cross-poll deferral coverage for backup
   (caught the executor-wrapping gap during cycle).
3. **AC3 symmetric — backup blocking kbSync**: proves backup is a valid blocker too
   (the heavy-set-membership half of AC1).
4. **AC4 — dream deferred by summary**: umbrella AC2 gap fill (no per-class dream
   deferral test existed prior).
5. **AC6 — primary-dev-sync deferred by summary**: umbrella AC2 gap fill.

## Test evidence

- npm run test-unit -- test/playwright/unit/ai/daemons/Orchestrator.spec.mjs -> 17/17 passed in 986ms
  - 12 baseline tests unchanged + 5 new
- Existing summary<->kbSync coverage at lines 134 + 184 unchanged
- Existing golden-path dream-dependency tests at lines 222-302 unchanged (golden-path's
  separate light-maintenance contract per #11511 / #11512 is independent of this change)

## Out of scope

- Lane D (PrimaryRepoSyncService.runKbSync() nested cascade observability) — follows
  as separate small lane after V-B-A trace
- Lane E (observability / stale-lease health surfaces) — separate ticket
- HeavyMaintenanceLeaseService consumer-side JSDoc (friction-to-gold from PR #11509
  cycle-1+cycle-2) — separate small follow-up per @neo-gpt routing

Co-authored-by: tobiu <tobiasuhlig78@gmail.com>"
- 2026-05-17T06:23:11Z @tobiu referenced in commit `55dadc7` - "feat(ai): annotate runKbSync cascade as kbSync lifecycle (#11520) (#11521)

* feat(ai): annotate runKbSync cascade as kbSync lifecycle — Lane D narrow of #11503 (#11520)

Closes Lane D narrow observability of #11503 umbrella (AC8: nested KB cascade no
longer hidden as primary-dev-sync only). Sibling to closed Lanes B (#11506) and
C (#11509), and in-flight Lanes A (PR #11514) and cross-daemon (#11519).

## Code change

PrimaryRepoSyncService.runKbSync() now annotates the cascade as a first-class
kbSync task lifecycle event:
- taskStateService.markStarted('kbSync', 'cascaded-from-primary-dev-sync') before spawn
- markCompleted on success / markFailed(taskName, e.status||1) on cascade failure
- healthService.recordTaskOutcome('kbSync', 'running'|'completed'|'failed',
  {parent: 'primary-dev-sync', reason, ...}) at lifecycle points
- Cascade failure rethrows to preserve current caller semantics
- Both services optional-chained for backward compatibility

Threading: orchestrator's primary-dev-sync invocation already passes both
services into PrimaryRepoSyncService.runTask. Added the pass-through chain
through syncPrimaryDev -> syncConfiguredDevRoots / syncDevRoot /
resolveMetaAndPull -> runKbSync. The singular syncConfiguredDevRoot path
(non-primary roots) passes runKbSync: false so the cascade never fires from
that path and services threading would be dead-weight.

## Out of scope

- Cross-daemon coverage + lease-inheritance contract: #11519 (separate ticket,
  self-claimed; queued post-merge of #11514/#11518)
- TaskStateService / HealthService schema changes: none needed; existing
  details-object on recordTaskOutcome carries arbitrary {parent} field
- Cascade-as-separate-task-class in TaskDefinitions: rejected — semantically
  IS the same kbSync class; provenance distinction is the annotation's job

## Test evidence

- npm run test-unit -- test/playwright/unit/ai/daemons/services/PrimaryRepoSyncService.spec.mjs
  -> 16/16 passed in 690ms (12 baseline + 4 new covering AC1-AC4 + AC8)
- npm run test-unit -- test/playwright/unit/ai/daemons/Orchestrator.spec.mjs
  -> 12/12 passed (baseline; will become 17/17 after PR #11514 Lane A merges)

The 4 new spec tests cover:
1. Success path: markStarted + markCompleted + 2 recordTaskOutcome events with parent annotation
2. Failure path: markStarted + markFailed(code) + 2 recordTaskOutcome events; cascade error rethrown
3. Backward-compat: no services injected -> pure shell-out, no annotation, no throw
4. Custom parentTaskName: annotation reason + details.parent adapt to parentTaskName option

## Authority anchors

- Parent umbrella: #11503 (Enforce heavy-maintenance mutex across Agent OS tasks),
  AC8 explicit
- Sibling lane (closed): #11505 / PR #11506 (Lane B - lease primitive)
- Sibling lane (closed): #11507 / PR #11509 (Lane C - manual CLI adoption)
- Sibling lane (in flight): #11513 / PR #11514 (Lane A - backup heavy-maintenance)
- Sibling lane (queued): #11519 (cross-daemon coverage + env-var inheritance)
- Lead-call: @neo-gpt MESSAGE:fb84293e-325b-4f0f-bf76-473d9ad342ff (narrow Lane D
  is observability, NOT coverage; coverage was transitively closed by Lane C)
- V-B-A trace: #11503 comment IC_kwDODSospM8AAAABClwhYQ (cascade DOES route
  through Lane C-wrapped script; observability is the remaining gap)

(#11520)

* fix(ai): address PR #11521 cycle 1 — strict temporal ordering test + threaded-option JSDoc (#11520)

Per @neo-gpt cycle 1 review (PRR_kwDODSospM8AAAABAJRHVA), two narrow Required Actions:

1. **Strengthen success-path test for AC6 temporal ordering**: previous test asserted
   end-state of `events` and `outcomes` arrays but did NOT verify WHEN annotations fire
   relative to execFileSyncFn. AC6 requires markStarted + running outcome BEFORE exec
   begins; markCompleted + completed outcome AFTER exec returns. Without ordering pin,
   a future refactor moving annotations around exec could pass end-state tests while
   violating the observability lifecycle contract.

   Test rewritten with a strict call-sequence array — instrumented helpers push
   synchronous markers on every call (state-started / health-running / exec /
   state-completed / health-completed); assertion is on exact sequence equality.
   Fail-mode: any reordering of the 5-step sequence breaks the test.

2. **Add JSDoc for threaded service options**: the `taskStateService` and
   `healthService` option-bag pass-through was added to `syncPrimaryDev()`,
   `syncConfiguredDevRoots()`, `syncDevRoot()`, and `resolveMetaAndPull()` in
   commit 29abe3a10 but @param entries were not added. Added on all 4 methods
   with optional-chained semantics + Lane D narrow attribution.

No runtime behavior change. Test-strengthening + JSDoc only.

Test evidence:
- npm run test-unit -- test/playwright/unit/ai/daemons/services/PrimaryRepoSyncService.spec.mjs
  -> 16/16 passed in 781ms (12 baseline + 4 new; strict-ordering test is now the
  load-bearing #11520 AC6 assertion)

(#11520)
